cards.front: Why not block the event loop?
cards.back: JS is single-threaded; a long sync task freezes all requests.
CJS: require, sync. ESM: import, async, static analysis.
Process data in chunks with backpressure instead of all in memory.
nextTick runs before the next phase (can starve I/O); setImmediate in the check phase.
Reads top-down, errors via try/catch, no callback hell.
json lists wanted ranges; lock pins exact versions for reproducible installs.
Read from process.env (often a .env file); never hardcode them.
npm installs/manages; npx runs a package binary without global install.
(err, result) — check err first. Modern code prefers promises.
The current module's folder path — build reliable file paths with it.
Named commands in package.json run with npm run <name> — your task shortcuts.

cards.front: What do ^ and ~ mean in versions?
cards.back: ^1.2.3 allows minor+patch updates; ~1.2.3 allows patch only.
It's large and rebuildable from package.json/lock — commit those, not the folder.
readFileSync blocks the loop; prefer async fs.promises in servers.
parse turns a JSON string into an object; stringify does the reverse.
It builds paths with the right separator on every OS — don't hardcode "/".

cards.front: Use all CPU cores in Node?
cards.back: One process uses one core; use cluster or worker_threads to scale CPU work.
0 means success, non-zero means failure — scripts and CI rely on it.
An unhandled rejection can crash the process; always .catch or try/catch await.
A convention env var ("production"/"development") to switch behavior and optimizations.
exports is a shortcut alias; reassigning it breaks the link — set module.exports.
A fixed chunk of raw binary bytes — Node's way to handle files, network data, etc.
deps are needed at runtime; devDeps (test, build tools) are only for development.
